text: The surgeon attempts to access the preperitoneal pocket, carefully pulling down the tissue while intermittently lifting the flap to visually inspect the underlying colon for any signs of distress or abnormality.
The surgeon extends the medial dissection further than usual for large inguinal scrotal hernias, taking down the flap to improve visualization on both sides of the defect.
The surgeon observes the chronic hernia sac, which appears to be completely fused to the ilio-pubic tract below.
The surgeon examines a thickened hernia sac, which is visibly thicker than average, indicating the hernia has been present for several years.
The hernia sac is prominently visible as the surgeon sweeps surrounding tissue away, searching for the vas deferens. The sigmoid colon is positioned lower in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates around the corner, encountering a significant amount of fat over the iliac vessels. The hernia sac is lifted at multiple points, revealing small filmy attachments that are being carefully assessed.

The surgeon places the mesh perfectly within the surgical site, securing it with high lateral fixation above the myopectenial orifice. The fixation is positioned medial to the main coronavorous vein, allowing the mesh to curl around the triangle of doom to prevent recurrence.
The surgeon performs a supermedial fixation as part of the standard closure technique, securing the mesh in place within the surgical field.
